expanded_description: When a transversal intersects two or more lines, it creates eight angles classified by their positions relative to the lines and the transversal. Understanding these angle pair classifications—corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, and consecutive interior—is the essential foundation for all work with parallel lines, angle relationships, and geometric proofs involving transversals.

core_concept.principle: A transversal intersecting two coplanar lines creates eight angles organized into specific pairs based on their positions. These pairs are: corresponding angles (same position at each intersection), alternate interior angles (between the lines, opposite sides of transversal), alternate exterior angles (outside the lines, opposite sides), and consecutive interior angles (between the lines, same side). Each classification determines a specific geometric relationship when the lines are parallel.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a transversal as a road crossing two other roads. At each intersection, the road creates four angles. Comparing angles at the two intersections reveals patterns: angles in the same position (like the upper-left corner at both intersections) are corresponding. Angles between the two roads on opposite sides of the crossing road are alternate interior. Angles between the two roads on the same side are consecutive interior. These position-based names are the vocabulary for everything that follows—once you can identify the angle pair, you can apply the correct theorem.

image.alt: Two horizontal lines intersected by a diagonal transversal. All eight angles numbered 1-8. Four angle pair types highlighted with different colors: corresponding angles (same color, positions 1&5, 2&6, 3&7, 4&8), alternate interior (Z-pattern, 3&6, 4&5), alternate exterior (outside Z, 1&8, 2&7), consecutive interior (C-pattern, 3&5, 4&6). A legend identifies each type.
image.caption: Angle pairs formed by a transversal: corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, and consecutive interior.

sections.introduction: A transversal is a line that intersects two or more coplanar lines at distinct points. This intersection creates eight angles, each with a specific position relative to the intersected lines and the transversal. Learning to classify these angles by position is the first and most important skill for understanding parallel line relationships.

introduction_image.alt: A simple diagram: two horizontal lines m and n, intersected by a diagonal transversal t. The eight angles are numbered 1 through 8 in standard order. Lines m and n are labeled. The regions between the lines (interior) and outside the lines (exterior) are shaded differently. The transversal t is highlighted.
introduction_image.caption: Lines m and n with transversal t. Eight angles are formed, four at each intersection.

key_concept.definition: A transversal is a line that intersects two or more coplanar lines at different points. At each intersection, the transversal creates four angles. The eight total angles are classified by their positions relative to the two intersected lines (interior or exterior) and their positions relative to the transversal (same side or opposite sides).
key_concept.context: This vocabulary is the foundation for the Corresponding Angles Postulate and all parallel line theorems. Without precise classification of angle pairs, it is impossible to correctly apply the theorems that relate these angles. Mastering these terms is the prerequisite for Sections 3.2-3.5.

image.alt: Detailed diagram: lines m and n with transversal t. Interior region shaded (between m and n). Exterior regions labeled (above m, below n). Each angle labeled with both its number and whether it is interior or exterior. The two intersection points are marked.
image.caption: Interior angles: ∠3, ∠4, ∠5, ∠6 (between the lines). Exterior angles: ∠1, ∠2, ∠7, ∠8 (outside the lines).

process_flow.process: Identify the Two Intersected Lines and the Transversal
process_flow.description: Determine which two lines are being studied (often labeled m and n, or ℓ₁ and ℓ₂) and which line is the transversal (often labeled t). The transversal is the line that crosses the other two.
process_flow.mechanism: Look for three lines in the diagram. The two lines that appear to be 'parallel' or are the focus of the problem are the intersected lines. The line cutting across them is the transversal. There are two intersection points, each creating four angles.
process_flow.result: Clear identification of which line is the transversal and which two lines it intersects.
applications: In a diagram with lines m, n, and t where t crosses both m and n: m and n are the intersected lines, t is the transversal.
If three lines intersect pairwise, you may need to treat different lines as the transversal for different angle pairs.
The eight angles are formed: 4 at the intersection of t with m, 4 at the intersection of t with n.

image.alt: Diagram with lines m, n, and t. t is highlighted as the transversal. m and n are highlighted as the intersected lines. The two intersection points are circled. Labels: 'Transversal = t (crosses m and n),' 'Intersected lines = m and n.'
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the transversal and the two lines it intersects.

process_flow.description: Number the angles from 1 to 8 in a standard order. Typically, angles 1-4 are at the first intersection (clockwise or counterclockwise), and angles 5-8 are in the corresponding positions at the second intersection.
process_flow.mechanism: Standard numbering: At the upper intersection, number angles 1 (upper left), 2 (upper right), 3 (lower right), 4 (lower left) going clockwise or counterclockwise. At the lower intersection, number angles 5-8 in the exact same positions: 5 matches position of 1, 6 matches 2, 7 matches 3, 8 matches 4. This numbering makes corresponding angle pairs easy to see.
process_flow.result: Eight angles numbered consistently, making angle pair identification straightforward.
applications: Corresponding pairs are then: (1,5), (2,6), (3,7), (4,8) — matching numbers modulo 4.
This standard numbering is used consistently across textbooks and problems.
If angles are not numbered, use position descriptions (upper left, lower right, etc.).

image.alt: Diagram with all eight angles numbered 1-8 in standard positions. At top intersection: ∠1 (upper left), ∠2 (upper right), ∠3 (lower right), ∠4 (lower left). At bottom intersection: ∠5 (upper left), ∠6 (upper right), ∠7 (lower right), ∠8 (lower left). The matching positions highlighted.
image.caption: Step 2: Number angles 1-8 in consistent positions at both intersections.

process_flow.process: Classify Each Angle Pair by Position
process_flow.description: Determine whether an angle pair is corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, or consecutive interior based on their positions relative to the intersected lines and the transversal.
process_flow.mechanism: Use position criteria: (1) Interior vs. Exterior — angles between the intersected lines are interior (∠3,4,5,6); angles outside are exterior (∠1,2,7,8). (2) Same side vs. Opposite sides of transversal — compare the two angles' positions relative to the transversal. (3) Combine: Corresponding = same position, different intersection. Alternate Interior = interior + opposite sides. Alternate Exterior = exterior + opposite sides. Consecutive Interior = interior + same side.
process_flow.result: Correct classification of any angle pair formed by the transversal.
applications: Alternate Interior: (3,6), (4,5) — between lines, opposite sides of transversal.
Alternate Exterior: (1,8), (2,7) — outside lines, opposite sides of transversal.
Consecutive Interior: (3,5), (4,6) — between lines, same side of transversal.

image.alt: Four small diagrams, each highlighting a different angle pair type: (1) Corresponding angles highlighted in matching colors. (2) Alternate interior with Z-pattern. (3) Alternate exterior with outside Z-pattern. (4) Consecutive interior with C-pattern. Each with the angle numbers and classification label.
image.caption: Step 3: Classify angle pairs by position — corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, or consecutive interior.

classifications.value: Angles in the same relative position at each intersection. They are on the same side of the transversal and both either above or below their respective intersected line.
characteristics: Same side of the transversal
Both above their line OR both below their line
Visual pattern: same 'corner' at each intersection
classifications.behavior: Corresponding angles are the most important pair type. The Corresponding Angles Postulate (if lines are parallel, corresponding angles are congruent) is the foundational postulate from which all other parallel line angle theorems are proved.

examples.explanation:
∠1 is in the upper-left position at the top intersection.

∠5 is in the upper-left position at the bottom intersection.

∠1 and ∠5 are corresponding angles — same position, different intersections.

If the lines are parallel, these angles are congruent.

image.alt: Diagram highlighting ∠1 and ∠5 in the same color (both upper-left positions). ∠2 and ∠6 in another color (both upper-right). ∠3 and ∠7 in a third color (both lower-right). ∠4 and ∠8 in a fourth color (both lower-left). All four pairs shown.
image.caption: Four corresponding angle pairs: same color = same position = corresponding.

classifications.value: Angles between the two intersected lines (interior) and on opposite sides of the transversal.
characteristics: Two pairs: (∠3, ∠6) and (∠4, ∠5)
Both angles are interior (between the lines)
On opposite sides of the transversal
Visual pattern: forms a 'Z' or zigzag shape
classifications.behavior: When lines are parallel, alternate interior angles are congruent. This theorem is proved using the Corresponding Angles Postulate and the Vertical Angles Theorem. The Z-pattern is a quick visual identifier.

examples.process: The Z-pattern for alternate interior angles
examples.explanation:
∠3 is between the lines, to the right of the transversal (at top intersection).

∠6 is between the lines, to the left of the transversal (at bottom intersection).

They are interior and on opposite sides — alternate interior angles.

Trace a Z starting at ∠3, down the transversal, across to ∠6.

image.alt: Diagram with ∠3 and ∠6 highlighted in matching colors. A bold Z-shaped line traces the path from ∠3 down the transversal, then across the lower line to ∠6. Label: 'Z-pattern = Alternate Interior Angles.'
image.caption: Alternate interior angles form a Z-pattern: ∠3 and ∠6, ∠4 and ∠5.

classifications.value: Angles outside the two intersected lines (exterior) and on opposite sides of the transversal.
characteristics: Two pairs: (∠1, ∠8) and (∠2, ∠7)
Both angles are exterior (outside the lines)
On opposite sides of the transversal
Visual pattern: similar to alternate interior but outside
classifications.behavior: When lines are parallel, alternate exterior angles are congruent. This theorem is proved similarly to the alternate interior angles theorem, using corresponding angles and vertical angles.

examples.explanation:
∠1 is outside the lines, to the left of the transversal (at top).

∠8 is outside the lines, to the right of the transversal (at bottom).

They are exterior and on opposite sides — alternate exterior angles.

If lines are parallel, m∠1 = m∠8.

image.alt: Diagram with ∠1 and ∠8 highlighted in matching colors. They are positioned outside the lines on opposite sides of the transversal. A curved arrow connects them. Label: 'Alternate Exterior Angles — exterior, opposite sides.'
image.caption: Alternate exterior angles: ∠1 and ∠8, ∠2 and ∠7. Exterior and opposite sides.

classifications.value: Angles between the two intersected lines (interior) and on the SAME side of the transversal.
characteristics: Two pairs: (∠3, ∠5) and (∠4, ∠6)
Both angles are interior (between the lines)
On the SAME side of the transversal
Visual pattern: forms a 'C' or 'U' shape
classifications.behavior: When lines are parallel, consecutive interior angles are SUPPLEMENTARY (sum to 180°). This is a sum relationship, not equality. The C-pattern is a quick visual identifier. This pair is unique among the four types because it is the only one that is supplementary rather than congruent.

examples.process: The C-pattern for consecutive interior angles
examples.explanation:
∠3 is between the lines, to the right of the transversal (at top).

∠5 is between the lines, to the right of the transversal (at bottom).

They are interior and on the SAME side — consecutive interior angles.

If lines are parallel: m∠3 + m∠5 = 180°.

image.alt: Diagram with ∠3 and ∠5 highlighted in matching colors. A bold C-shaped line traces the path from ∠3 down the transversal and across to ∠5. Equation: m∠3 + m∠5 = 180°. Label: 'C-pattern = Consecutive Interior Angles (Supplementary).'
image.caption: Consecutive interior angles form a C-pattern: ∠3+∠5 = 180°, ∠4+∠6 = 180°.

image.alt: Complete reference diagram: All four angle pair types displayed on a single transversal diagram. A summary table: Angle Pair Type, Pairs, Visual Pattern, Relationship when Parallel. Corresponding: (1,5)(2,6)(3,7)(4,8), same corner, CONGRUENT. Alternate Interior: (3,6)(4,5), Z-pattern, CONGRUENT. Alternate Exterior: (1,8)(2,7), outside Z, CONGRUENT. Consecutive Interior: (3,5)(4,6), C-pattern, SUPPLEMENTARY.
image.caption: Complete angle pair classification reference: names, pairs, patterns, and relationships.

sections.section_title: Interior vs. Exterior: A Fundamental Distinction
sections.introduction: The first question to ask about any angle formed by a transversal is: Is the angle between the two intersected lines (interior) or outside them (exterior)? This distinction is the foundation for all angle pair classifications and must be mastered before moving on to specific pair types.

introduction_image.alt: Two horizontal lines with a transversal. The region between the two lines is shaded in light blue and labeled 'INTERIOR.' The regions above the top line and below the bottom line are unshaded and labeled 'EXTERIOR.' Angles 3,4,5,6 are circled and labeled 'Interior Angles.' Angles 1,2,7,8 are circled and labeled 'Exterior Angles.'
introduction_image.caption: Interior region = between the lines. Exterior regions = outside the lines. Each angle is classified by its location.

key_concept.definition: Interior angles lie in the region between the two intersected lines. Exterior angles lie in the regions outside the two intersected lines. This distinction depends only on position relative to the intersected lines, not on the transversal. Angles 3, 4, 5, and 6 are interior. Angles 1, 2, 7, and 8 are exterior.
key_concept.context: The interior/exterior classification is the first filter for angle pair identification. Corresponding angles can mix interior and exterior; alternate interior and consecutive interior are exclusively interior; alternate exterior is exclusively exterior. Knowing whether an angle is interior or exterior immediately narrows the possible pair types.

image.alt: Clear diagram: Two parallel lines, transversal. Interior angles (3,4,5,6) highlighted with a shaded band between the lines. Exterior angles (1,2,7,8) highlighted outside the band. Each angle labeled with I (interior) or E (exterior).
image.caption: Interior = between the lines (∠3,4,5,6). Exterior = outside the lines (∠1,2,7,8).

classifications.type: How to Determine Interior vs. Exterior
classifications.value: Look at the two intersected lines (not the transversal). The region between them is interior; everything else is exterior.
characteristics: Imagine the two intersected lines extended infinitely — they divide the plane into three horizontal strips
The strip between the lines is the interior region
The strips above the top line and below the bottom line are exterior regions
Each angle's position determines whether it is interior or exterior
classifications.behavior: This classification is independent of whether the lines are parallel. Even if the lines intersect (forming a triangle with the transversal), the concept of interior vs. exterior still applies, though the classification of angle pairs becomes more complex.

examples.explanation:
∠3: below top line, above bottom line → interior.

∠4: below top line, above bottom line → interior.

∠1: above top line → exterior.

∠7: below bottom line → exterior.

The transversal side (left/right) does not affect interior/exterior status.

image.alt: Diagram with each angle individually tested: a thought bubble 'Between the lines?' with Yes→Interior, No→Exterior. Angles 3,4,5,6 marked Yes (Interior). Angles 1,2,7,8 marked No (Exterior).
image.caption: Simple test: 'Is the angle between the two intersected lines?' Yes = Interior. No = Exterior.

image.alt: Summary diagram showing the three regions: upper exterior, interior, lower exterior. All eight angles placed in their regions. A flowchart: Angle → Between the lines? → Yes (Interior) → No (Exterior). Used in combination with transversal side to determine full classification.
image.caption: Interior vs. Exterior: the first classification step for any angle formed by a transversal.

sections.section_title: Same Side vs. Opposite Sides of the Transversal
sections.introduction: After determining whether angles are interior or exterior, the next classification step is whether they lie on the same side or opposite sides of the transversal. This distinction separates corresponding and consecutive interior (same side) from alternate interior and alternate exterior (opposite sides).

introduction_image.alt: A transversal dividing the diagram into left and right halves. Left side highlighted blue, right side highlighted yellow. ∠1,∠4,∠5,∠8 are on the right side. ∠2,∠3,∠6,∠7 are on the left side. Labels explain: 'Same side = both in same colored region. Opposite sides = one in blue, one in yellow.'
introduction_image.caption: The transversal divides the plane into two half-planes. Angles on the same side share a half-plane; angles on opposite sides do not.

key_concept.definition: The transversal acts as a dividing line. Angles on the same side of the transversal lie in the same half-plane created by the transversal. Angles on opposite sides lie in different half-planes. This classification, combined with interior/exterior, uniquely identifies each angle pair type.
key_concept.context: Combining the two binary classifications (interior/exterior and same/opposite side) creates a 2×2 decision matrix that perfectly identifies all four angle pair types. This systematic approach prevents misclassification.

image.alt: A 2×2 decision matrix: Rows = Interior vs. Exterior. Columns = Same Side vs. Opposite Sides. Cells: (Interior, Same Side) = Consecutive Interior. (Interior, Opposite Sides) = Alternate Interior. (Exterior, Same Side) = Corresponding (mixed with interior). (Exterior, Opposite Sides) = Alternate Exterior. Note that corresponding angles pair an exterior with an interior.
image.caption: Decision matrix: Interior/Exterior × Same/Opposite Side identifies every angle pair type.

classifications.value: A systematic method for classifying any angle pair by answering two questions.
characteristics: Question 1: Is the angle interior or exterior? (Between the lines or outside?)
Question 2: Relative to the other angle, are they on the same side or opposite sides of the transversal?
Interior + Opposite = Alternate Interior
Exterior + Opposite = Alternate Exterior
Interior + Same = Consecutive Interior
Mixed interior/exterior + Same = Corresponding
classifications.behavior: This matrix eliminates guesswork. Instead of memorizing which angle numbers form which pairs, you can determine the classification analytically from position. This is especially useful when angle numbering is non-standard or absent.

examples.process: Using the matrix to classify ∠3 and ∠6
examples.explanation:
∠3: Is it interior? YES (between the lines).

∠6: Is it interior? YES (between the lines).

Are they on the same side of the transversal? ∠3 is on the right, ∠6 is on the left — OPPOSITE sides.

Matrix result: Interior + Opposite = ALTERNATE INTERIOR.

image.alt: The decision matrix with ∠3 and ∠6 analyzed. An arrow traces: Interior? Yes → Opposite sides? Yes → Result: Alternate Interior. The angle pair is highlighted in the diagram. Another example analyzing ∠2 and ∠6: Interior? No (∠2 is exterior) → Same side? Yes → Result: Corresponding.
image.caption: Decision matrix in action: ∠3 and ∠6 → Alternate Interior. ∠2 and ∠6 → Corresponding.

image.alt: Complete classification flowchart: Start → Is the angle Interior or Exterior? → Interior or Exterior? → Are the two angles on the Same side or Opposite sides? → Final classification. Each path illustrated with a diagram example.
image.caption: Classification flowchart: two questions uniquely determine any angle pair type.

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes in Angle Pair Identification
sections.introduction: Angle pair classification is precise but prone to error when learners rely on memorized number patterns rather than positional reasoning. Common mistakes include confusing alternate interior with consecutive interior, misidentifying corresponding angles when numbering is non-standard, and classifying angles without checking both the interior/exterior status and the same/opposite side status.

key_concept.definition: Common classification errors include: confusing the Z-pattern (alternate interior, congruent) with the C-pattern (consecutive interior, supplementary), miscounting angle numbers when the diagram uses non-standard numbering, and assuming two angles are related because they 'look equal' without classifying their positional relationship first.
key_concept.context: These errors lead to applying the wrong theorem—claiming angles are congruent when they are actually supplementary, or vice versa. Since parallel line problems often ask for angle measures, a classification error produces a wrong numerical answer and a flawed proof.

image.alt: Four warning panels: (1) Z-pattern vs C-pattern confusion — Alternate Interior (Z, congruent) vs Consecutive Interior (C, supplementary). (2) Non-standard angle numbering — relying on memorized numbers instead of position. (3) Assuming relationships without classifying — 'they look equal so they must be corresponding.' (4) Forgetting that corresponding angles pair interior with exterior.
image.caption: Common classification errors: pattern confusion, numbering reliance, assumptions, and interior/exterior mix-up.

classifications.type: Confusing Z-Pattern (Alternate Interior) with C-Pattern (Consecutive Interior)
classifications.value: Both involve interior angles. The difference is whether they are on opposite sides (Z, congruent) or the same side (C, supplementary).
characteristics: Alternate Interior: angles are on OPPOSITE sides → Z-pattern → CONGRUENT
Consecutive Interior: angles are on the SAME side → C-pattern → SUPPLEMENTARY (180°)
Visual test: 'Does the path from one angle to the other cross the transversal?' Yes → alternate. No → consecutive.
Mixing these up is the single most common error in parallel line problems
classifications.behavior: The Z-pattern crosses the transversal; the C-pattern stays on one side. When tracing from one angle to the other, if your finger crosses the transversal, the angles are alternate (congruent). If it doesn't, they are consecutive (supplementary).

examples.process: Finger-trace test for Z vs. C
examples.explanation:
∠3 to ∠6: Start at ∠3 (right side), go down the transversal (crossing? No, you're on the transversal), then go left to ∠6. The path from ∠3 to ∠6 CROSSES the interior region from right to left — this is a Z-pattern. Alternate Interior. Congruent.

∠3 to ∠5: Start at ∠3 (right side), go down to ∠5 (right side). No crossing of sides. This is a C-pattern. Consecutive Interior. Supplementary.

image.alt: Two diagrams side by side: (1) Z-pattern highlighting ∠3 and ∠6 with a bold Z traced. Label: 'Crosses the transversal → Alternate Interior → CONGRUENT.' (2) C-pattern highlighting ∠3 and ∠5 with a bold C traced. Label: 'Same side → Consecutive Interior → SUPPLEMENTARY.'
image.caption: Z crosses sides → Alternate Interior (≅). C stays on same side → Consecutive Interior (180°).

classifications.note: Solution: Always check 'same side or opposite sides of the transversal?' before applying any relationship. This one question prevents the most common error in parallel line geometry.

classifications.value: Assuming that specific angle number pairs (like 3 and 6) are always alternate interior, regardless of how the diagram is numbered.
characteristics: Error: '∠3 and ∠6 are always alternate interior' — only true if the diagram uses standard numbering
Different textbooks or problems may number angles differently
Some diagrams don't number angles at all, requiring positional reasoning
Correct: Learn to classify by position, not by memorized number pairs
classifications.behavior: Angle numbers are arbitrary labels. The geometric relationship depends on position, not the number. A diagram might number angles clockwise instead of counterclockwise, or start numbering from a different position, completely changing which numbers correspond.
examples: Non-standard numbering: Angles numbered 1-4 at top going clockwise starting from upper right. Angles 5-8 at bottom going counterclockwise. The memorized pairs no longer work. The student must analyze position.
The safe approach: 'What position is each angle in? Interior/exterior? Same/opposite side?' This works for any numbering or no numbering at all.
classifications.note: Solution: Use the decision matrix (interior/exterior × same/opposite side) instead of memorizing number pairs. Position-based reasoning works universally.

image.alt: Summary of common mistakes with corrected approaches. Each error shown with a diagram, the incorrect thinking, and the correct positional analysis.
image.caption: Avoiding classification errors: use positional reasoning, not memorized patterns.

sections.introduction: Fluency in angle pair identification requires practice with various diagrams, numbering systems, and problem contexts. Work through these problems to develop quick, accurate classification skills.

worked_examples.title: Angle Pair Identification Practice
worked_examples.problem:
Use the standard numbered diagram (m, n, transversal t, angles 1-8 in standard positions). Classify each pair and state the relationship if lines are parallel.

1) ∠2 and ∠6
2) ∠4 and ∠5
3) ∠1 and ∠7
4) ∠3 and ∠6
5) ∠4 and ∠6
6) ∠1 and ∠5

step_by_step.explanation:
Position: Both are upper-right corners at their respective intersections. Same relative position.

Classification: Corresponding Angles.

Relationship if m ∥ n: Congruent (equal measure).

Interior/Exterior: ∠4 is interior (lower left at top). ∠5 is interior (upper left at bottom). Both interior.

Same/Opposite sides: ∠4 is on the left side. ∠5 is on the right side. Opposite sides.

Classification: Alternate Interior Angles.

Relationship if m ∥ n: Congruent.

Interior/Exterior: ∠1 is exterior (upper left at top). ∠7 is exterior (lower right at bottom). Both exterior.

Same/Opposite sides: ∠1 is on the left. ∠7 is on the right. Opposite sides.

Classification: Alternate Exterior Angles.

Relationship if m ∥ n: Congruent.

Interior/Exterior: Both interior.

Same/Opposite sides: ∠3 is on the right. ∠6 is on the left. Opposite sides.

Classification: Alternate Interior Angles.

Relationship if m ∥ n: Congruent.

Interior/Exterior: Both interior.

Same/Opposite sides: ∠4 is on the left. ∠6 is on the left. SAME side.

Classification: Consecutive Interior Angles (Same-Side Interior).

Relationship if m ∥ n: Supplementary (sum = 180°).

Position: Both are upper-left corners. Same relative position.

Classification: Corresponding Angles.

Relationship if m ∥ n: Congruent.

image.alt: Six diagrams, each highlighting the angle pair from the corresponding problem. Each with classification label and the relationship (congruent or supplementary). Color-coded by pair type.
image.caption: Angle pair identification practice: applying the decision matrix to classify six different pairs.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Corresponding — Congruent
2) Alternate Interior — Congruent
3) Alternate Exterior — Congruent
4) Alternate Interior — Congruent
5) Consecutive Interior — Supplementary (180°)
6) Corresponding — Congruent
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic classification of angle pairs using interior/exterior status and same/opposite sides of the transversal.

summary: When a transversal intersects two lines, eight angles are formed and classified into four pair types based on position. Key points: 1) Interior vs. Exterior: Interior angles lie between the two intersected lines (∠3,4,5,6). Exterior angles lie outside (∠1,2,7,8). 2) Same Side vs. Opposite Sides: The transversal divides the plane into two half-planes. Angles on the same side share a half-plane; angles on opposite sides do not. 3) Four Angle Pair Types: Corresponding angles — same position at different intersections (congruent when lines parallel). Alternate Interior — interior, opposite sides (congruent). Alternate Exterior — exterior, opposite sides (congruent). Consecutive Interior — interior, same side (supplementary, sum 180°). 4) Visual Patterns: Z-pattern = Alternate Interior. C-pattern = Consecutive Interior. Same corner = Corresponding. 5) Decision Matrix: Classify by asking two questions: Interior or Exterior? Same side or Opposite sides? The answers uniquely identify the pair type. 6) Common Errors: Confusing Z (congruent) with C (supplementary), relying on memorized angle numbers instead of positional reasoning, and forgetting to check both interior/exterior and same/opposite side status. 7) This classification is the essential prerequisite for all parallel line theorems and proofs in subsequent sections.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Transversal Angle Pairs' in center. Branches: Interior vs. Exterior (between lines or outside), Same Side vs. Opposite (relative to transversal), Four Pair Types (Corresponding — same position ≅, Alternate Interior — interior opposite ≅, Alternate Exterior — exterior opposite ≅, Consecutive Interior — interior same 180°), Visual Patterns (Z, C, same corner), Decision Matrix (2×2 classification).
review_questions_image.caption: Lines and Angles Formed by a Transversal Review: angle pair classification system and visual patterns.
